title: Introduction to reactRouter
code: #remotes::install_github("lgnbhl/reactRouter") # development version  install.packages("reactRouter")

title: Introduction to reactRouter
text: can add URL pages Quarto document R shiny like :
code: library(reactRouter) library(reactRouter) library(htmltools)  RouterProvider(   Route(     path = "/",     element = htmltools::tags$div(       NavLink(to = "/", "Main"),       NavLink(to = "/analysis", "Analysis"),       Outlet()     ),     Route(index = TRUE, element = "Main content"),     Route(path = "analysis", element = "Analysis content")   ) ) #> Warning: The `reloadDocument` argument of `Link()` default is now FALSE as of #> reactRouter 0.1.2. #> ℹ The default of `reloadDocument` was TRUE in version 0.1.1. It is now FALSE. #> This warning is displayed once per session. #> Call `lifecycle::last_lifecycle_warnings()` to see where this warning was #> generated.

title: Introduction to reactRouter
text: minimal example using shiny.
code: library(shiny) library(reactRouter)  ui <- RouterProvider(   Route(     path = "/",     element = div(       NavLink(to = "/", "Main"),       shiny::br(),       NavLink(to = "/other", "Other"),       Outlet()     ),     Route(index = TRUE, element = uiOutput(outputId = "uiMain")),     Route(path = "other", element = uiOutput(outputId = "uiOther"))   ) )  server <- function(input, output, session) {   output$uiMain <- renderUI( { p("Content home") } )   output$uiOther <- renderUI( { p("Other content") }) }  shinyApp(ui = ui, server = server)

title: Introduction to reactRouter
text: minimal example using bslib.
code: library(reactRouter) library(bslib) library(htmltools)  RouterProvider(   reactRouter::Route(     path = "/",     element = bslib::page_navbar(       title = "reactRouter with bslib",       nav_item(         reactRouter::NavLink(           "Home",           to = "/"         )       ),       nav_item(         reactRouter::NavLink(           "Analysis",           to = "/analysis"         )       ),       reactRouter::Outlet()     ),     reactRouter::Route(       index = TRUE,       element = div(         tags$h3("Home page"),         p("A basic example of reactRouter with bslib.")       )     ),     reactRouter::Route(       path = "analysis",       element = "Content analysis"     ),     reactRouter::Route(path = "*", element = "Custom error 404")   ) )

title: Introduction to reactRouter
text: minimal example using shinyMaterialUI.  Find examples muiMaterial .
code: # remotes::install_github("lgnbhl/muiMaterial") library(muiMaterial)  RouterProvider(   Route(     path = "/",     element = Box(       sx = list(flexGrow = 1),       AppBar(         position = "static",         Toolbar(           Typography(             variant = "h6",             component = "div",             sx = list(mr = 1),             "shinyMaterialUI"           ),           NavLink(             to = "/",             Button(               color = "inherit",               "Home"             )           ),           NavLink(             to = "analysis",             Button(               color = "inherit",               "Analysis"             )           )         )       ),       Box(Outlet())     ),     reactRouter::Route(       index = TRUE,       element = Box("Home page", sx = list(p = 1))     ),     reactRouter::Route(       path = "analysis",       element = Box("Content analysis", sx = list(p = 1))     ),     reactRouter::Route(path = "*", element = "Error 404")   ) )

title: Introduction to reactRouter
code: # adapted from example of shiny.router # https://github.com/Appsilon/shiny.router/tree/main/examples/shiny_modules library(shiny) library(reactRouter)  # This creates UI for each page. page <- function(title, content, id) {   ns <- NS(id)   div(     titlePanel(title),     p(content),     textOutput(ns("click_me"))   ) }  # Both sample pages. root_page <- page("Home page", "Home page clicks", "root") second_page <- page("Other page", "Other page clicks", "second")  server_module <- function(id, clicks, power = 1) {   moduleServer(id, function(input, output, session) {     output$click_me <- renderText({       as.numeric(clicks())^power     })   }) }  # Create output for our router in main UI of Shiny app. ui <- RouterProvider(   Route(     path = "/",     element = div(       NavLink(to = "/", "Main"), br(),       NavLink(to = "/other", "Other"),       actionButton("clicks", "Click me!"),       Outlet()     ),     Route(index = TRUE, element = div(root_page)),     Route(path = "other", element = div(second_page))   ) )  # Plug router into Shiny server. server <- function(input, output, session) {   clicks <- reactive({     input$clicks   })   server_module("root", clicks = clicks, power = 1)   server_module("second", clicks = clicks, power = 2) }  # Run server in a standard way. shinyApp(ui, server)

title: Introduction to reactRouter
text: React Router provides client routing, can easily create multiple routes Quarto R markdown documents:
code: # code to run in a Quarto document # example adapted from: https://github.com/remix-run/react-router/tree/dev/examples/basic library(reactRouter) library(htmltools)  Layout <- div(   style = "border:1px solid black;", # add border just for the example   h1("Basic Example"),   tags$p(     paste0('This example demonstrates some of the core features of React Router         including nested reactRouter::Route(), reactRouter::Outlet(),         reactRouter::Link(), and using a "*" route (aka "splat route")         to render a "not found" page when someone visits an unrecognized URL.'     )   ),   # A "layout route" is a good place to put markup you want to   # share across all the pages on your site, like navigation.   tags$nav(     tags$ul(       tags$li(         reactRouter::Link(to = "/", "Home")       ),       tags$li(         reactRouter::Link(to = "/dashboard", "Dashboard")       ),       tags$li(         reactRouter::Link(to = "/nothing-here", "Nothing Here")       )     )   ),   tags$hr(),   # An <Outlet> renders whatever child route is currently active,   # so you can think about this <Outlet> as a placeholder for   # the child routes we defined above.   reactRouter::Outlet() )  RouterProvider(   Route(     path = "/",     element = Layout,     Route(       index = TRUE,       element = div(         tags$h2("Home"),         tags$p("Home content")       )     ),     Route(       path = "dashboard",       element = div(         tags$h2("Dashboard"),         tags$p("Dashboard here")       )     ),     # Using path="*"" means "match anything", so this route     # acts like a catch-all for URLs that we don't have explicit     # routes for.     Route(       path = "*",       element = div(         tags$h2("Nothing to see here!"),         tags$p(           Link(to = "/", "Go to the home page")         )       )     )   ) )

title: Introduction to reactRouter
text: minimal example using dynamic segments, .e. using Route(= ":id/*"). allow R Shiny update URL hashs, add reloadDocument = TRUE (default FALSE) NavLink().
code: library(shiny) library(reactRouter) library(bslib)  ui <- RouterProvider(   Route(     path = "/",     element = bslib::page(       Link(         to = "/",         h3("reactRouter with dynamic routes", class = "m-3"),         style = "text-decoration: none; color: black"       ),       Outlet()     ),     Route(       index = TRUE,       element = div(         NavLink(           to = "project/1/overview",           reloadDocument = TRUE,            "Project 1"         ),         tags$br(),         NavLink(           to = "project/2/overview",           reloadDocument = TRUE,           "Project 2"         )       )     ),     Route(       path = "project/:id/*",       element = div(         NavLink(           to = "overview",           reloadDocument = TRUE,           "Overview"         ),         tags$br(),         NavLink(           to = "analysis",           reloadDocument = TRUE,           "Analysis"         ),         Outlet()       ),       Route(         path = "overview",         element = uiOutput("uiOverview")       ),       Route(         path = "analysis",         element = uiOutput("uiAnalysis")       )     )   ) )  server <- function(input, output, session) {   output$uiOverview <- renderUI({     session$clientData$url_hash   })   output$uiAnalysis <- renderUI({     session$clientData$url_hash   }) }  shinyApp(ui, server)

title: Introduction to reactRouter
text: shiny.router implements custom hash routing shiny. brochure provide mechanism creating natively multi-page shiny applications (still WIP).

title: Introduction to reactRouter
text: reactRouter implements React Router v.6.30.0. info use React Router can found official website.

what: RouterProvider() — the simplest way to get started
title: Choosing a router
text: RouterProvider() unified wrapper covers common use cases type argument: type = "hash" (default) — hash-based routing (/#/). Works Shiny, Quarto, static HTML. Routes visible URL, users can bookmark pages use browser back/forward. type = "memory" — -memory routing. URL never changes. Ideal embedded widgets, multi-step wizards, multiple independent routers page. type = "browser" — HTML5 History API (/). recommended use R (see BrowserRouter ). lower-level functions (createHashRouter(), createMemoryRouter(), HashRouter(), MemoryRouter()) also available give control. described .
code: library(reactRouter) library(htmltools)  RouterProvider(   # type = "hash" by default — change to type = "memory" if needed   Route(     path = "/",     element = div(       tags$nav(         tags$ul(           tags$li(NavLink(to = "/",      "Home")),           tags$li(NavLink(to = "/about", "About"))         )       ),       tags$hr(),       Outlet()     ),     Route(index = TRUE,   element = div(h2("Home"),  p("Welcome."))),     Route(path = "about", element = div(h2("About"), p("About page.")))   ) )

what: createHashRouter() — recommended default for Shiny and static sites
title: Choosing a router
text: createHashRouter best default choice R Shiny apps static sites (Quarto, R Markdown HTML output). uses URL hash (# fragment) routing, routes like /#/visible address bar. means users can bookmark pages, share deep links, use browser back/forward buttons — without requiring server handles URL rewriting. works well Shiny: Hash changes (/#/) never trigger real HTTP request, Shiny’s server affected. session$clientData$url_hash updates reactively whenever route changes, letting server know current page. preferred HashRouter: Unlocks full data router API: loader, action, errorElement, useLoaderData, useNavigation, useFetcher, deferred data. model React Router actively developing; HashRouter legacy compatibility wrapper. Note: default reloadDocument = FALSE used React Router handles navigation client-side without triggering full page reload.
code: library(reactRouter) library(htmltools)  createHashRouter(   Route(     path = "/",     element = div(       tags$nav(         tags$ul(           tags$li(NavLink(to = "/",      "Home")),           tags$li(NavLink(to = "/about", "About"))         )       ),       tags$hr(),       Outlet()     ),     Route(index = TRUE,   element = div(h2("Home"),  p("Welcome."))),     Route(path = "about", element = div(h2("About"), p("About page.")))   ) )

title: Choosing a router
text: createMemoryRouter keeps routing state entirely memory. read modify browser URL , means: always starts /, regardless real URL . Navigation updates -memory location browser address bar change. Browser back/forward buttons bookmarks reflect current route. makes ideal internal-navigation — multi-step wizards, tabbed panels, UI route implementation detail rather something user see share. also right choice need multiple independent routers page (e.g. several routed widgets Shiny dashboard), since don’t touch URL therefore can’t conflict . createMemoryRouter works Shiny apps, static HTML pages (file://), Quarto output blocks. Choosing hash memory routers: short: route visible URL (bookmarks, deep links, back/forward), use createHashRouter. routing purely internal UI concern, use createMemoryRouter.
code: library(reactRouter) library(htmltools)  createMemoryRouter(   createRoutesFromElements(     Route(       path = "/",       element = div(         tags$nav(           tags$ul(             tags$li(NavLink(to = "/",      "Home")),             tags$li(NavLink(to = "/about", "About"))           )         ),         tags$hr(),         Outlet()       ),       Route(index = TRUE,   element = div(h2("Home"),  p("Welcome."))),       Route(path = "about", element = div(h2("About"), p("About page.")))     )   ) )

what: Legacy: HashRouter() and MemoryRouter() — component API
title: Choosing a router
text: HashRouter MemoryRouter older, component-based equivalents createHashRouter createMemoryRouter. remain fully supported package safe use existing code. However, RouterProvider() (createHashRouter / createMemoryRouter) preferred new code component-based API: support loader, action, errorElement, data router features. direction React Router developing toward. Use HashRouter MemoryRouter specific reason stay component API (e.g. migrating existing app incrementally).
code: # Legacy: component API — still works, but RouterProvider() is preferred library(shiny) library(reactRouter)  ui <- HashRouter(   NavLink(to = "/", reloadDocument = TRUE, "Home"), br(),   NavLink(to = "/other", reloadDocument = TRUE, "Other"),   Routes(     Route(path = "/",      element = uiOutput("uiHome")),     Route(path = "/other", element = uiOutput("uiOther"))   ) )  server <- function(input, output, session) {   url_hash <- reactive(session$clientData$url_hash)    output$uiHome  <- renderUI({ p("Home — hash: ",  url_hash()) })   output$uiOther <- renderUI({ p("Other — hash: ", url_hash()) }) }  shinyApp(ui, server)

title: Choosing a router
text: BrowserRouter uses HTML5 History API (pushState) clean URLs (e.g. /instead /#/). standard router full React web applications, well-suited use R package: Static files (file://): opening, URL path file system path (e.g. /C:/Users/.../file.html), /. route matches, app immediately shows 404. Shiny apps: Clean URLs like /require server rewrite paths back /. Shiny default. Additionally, client-side navigation reloadDocument = FALSE means Shiny server way know current route without extra JavaScript workarounds. Refreshing sub-route: Even served proper HTTP server, refreshing browser /404 unless server configured rewrite routes index.html. BrowserRouter works correctly full web application deployment (e.g. React served Node.js, Express, Nginx URL rewriting). scenario outside scope R package.

title: Choosing a router
text: Link() NavLink() accept reloadDocument prop controls navigation works. default FALSE, matching React Router’s default.

title: Choosing a router
text: React Router handles navigation entirely client side — swaps displayed route component without reloading page. correct behavior : Static sites Quarto documents — server re-initialize. Client-side navigation provides smooth, instant route switching. createMemoryRouter MemoryRouter — full page reload reset -memory routing state back /, breaking navigation entirely. Data router loaders actions — loader action functions execute client-side navigations. full page reload bypasses React Router loaders run.

title: Choosing a router
text: Clicking link triggers full page reload, user clicked regular <href>. necessary Shiny apps server-rendered content : Shiny’s server needs re-initialize read new URL hash render correct uiOutput()/renderUI() content. Without reload, server-rendered UI update properly since Shiny session started old route. Set reloadDocument = TRUE whenever routes contain server-rendered output (uiOutput, plotOutput, etc.):
code: NavLink(to = "/about", reloadDocument = TRUE, "About") Link(to = "/",         reloadDocument = TRUE, "Home")

title: Tutorial: Using reactRouter with rhino and shiny.fluent
text: tutorial demonstrates build dynamic Shiny application using reactRouter routing shiny.fluent modern UI components, within rhino framework. ’ll use Dota 2 API data example routing multiple pages. Initially, ensure necessary rhino package installed. can running following command R console: Next, need create new rhino project. haven’t already set rhino project, can running following command R console: create basic structure application. Add following libraries dependencies.R file: Now ready go.
code: # Install rhino if not yet installed install.packages("rhino") # Initialize a new rhino project (will create project scaffolding) rhino::init() # dependencies.R  library(rhino) # App structure library(httr) # API requests library(shiny.fluent) # UI components library(reactRouter) # Client-side routing library(echarts4r) # Charting library(stringdist) # String matching library(treesitter) # Optional: Syntax parsing library(treesitter.r) # Optional: R syntax support renv::snapshot()

title: Tutorial: Using reactRouter with rhino and shiny.fluent
text: example, create simple application displays information Dota 2 heroes. application multiple routes, allowing users navigate different pages. components application structured follows: home: main page application, display list heroes menu: navigation menu application, allowing users navigate different pages header: header component displayed every page details: page containing detailed information specific hero benchmark: page display benchmark statistics heroes ranks: page display ranks heroes based performance final strucutre app look like : main part application address routing UI components app.R file. function defines overall layout routing application using reactRouter. contains four key parts: Top-level routing via HashRouter() Routes(). Root Route /: Displays home page. Nested Route /:projectId/*: Displays layout (menu) child routes (details, benchmark, rank). sub-route renders different module UI (e.g., detailsui,benchmarkui, benchmarkui). Fallback * Route: Catches undefined paths shows custom 404 message.
code: ├── app │   ├── js │   │   └── index.js │   ├── logic │   │   ├── data.R │   │   └── utils.R │   ├── main.R │   ├── static │   │   ├── css │   │   │   └── app.min.css │   │   ├── favicon.ico │   │   └── js │   │       └── app.min.js │   ├── styles │   │   └── main.scss │   └── view │       ├── benchmark.R │       ├── details.R │       ├── header.R │       ├── home.R │       ├── menu.R │       └── rank.R ├── app.R ├── config.yml ├── dependencies.R ├── renv.lock ├── rhino.yml └── run_dev.R # app / main.R  box::use(   app / view / home,   app / view / menu,   app / view / details,   app / view / benchmark,   app / view / rank )   # Define UI with namespaced modules ui <- function(id) {   ns <- shiny::NS(id) # Namespace for module isolation   shiny.fluent::fluentPage(     reactRouter::HashRouter(       reactRouter::Routes(         # Home page route         reactRouter::Route(path = "/", element = home$ui(ns("home"))),          # Project-based nested routes         reactRouter::Route(           path = "/:projectId/*",           element = menu$ui(ns("menu")), # Common layout/menu           children = list(             reactRouter::Route(               path = "details",               element = details$ui(ns("details"))             ),             reactRouter::Route(               path = "benchmark",               element = benchmark$ui(ns("benchmark"))             ),             reactRouter::Route(               path = "rank",               element = rank$ui(ns("rank"))             )           )         ),          # Fallback for undefined routes         reactRouter::Route(path = "*", element = "Custom error 404")       )     )   ) }  #' @export server <- function(id) {   shiny::moduleServer(id, function(input, output, session) {     hero_selected <- home$server("home")      shiny::observe({       shiny::req(hero_selected())        print(paste0("hero_id selected: ", hero_selected()))     })      menu$server("menu", hero_selected = hero_selected)     details$server("details", hero_selected = hero_selected)     benchmark$server("benchmark", hero_selected = hero_selected)     rank$server("rank", hero_selected = hero_selected)   }) }

title: Tutorial: Using reactRouter with rhino and shiny.fluent
text: can now run app locally following script:
code: # run_dev.R  rhino::build_js() rhino::build_sass() shiny::runApp(port = 4929, launch.browser = FALSE)

title: Tutorial: Using reactRouter with rhino and shiny.fluent
text: tutorial, dynamic Shiny application using: rhino structured project setup. shiny.fluent modern interactive UI. reactRouter dynamic nested client-side routing. setup provides flexibility, scalability, maintainability complex Shiny applications.

title: Using useLoaderData
text: useLoaderData() connects React Router’s useLoaderData() hook UI. calls hook injects result component — either text content inside plain HTML element, structured JavaScript value prop shiny.react-based component. Use inside Route loader function.

title: Using useLoaderData
text: Pass htmltools tag render loader data text. simplest use case — extra configuration needed: Full example dynamic route parameters:
code: # Render a single field as text useLoaderData(tags$h3(), selector = "name")      # renders the "name" field useLoaderData(tags$pre())                         # renders full JSON string library(reactRouter) library(htmltools) library(dplyr)  people_json <- dplyr::starwars |>   mutate(id = dplyr::row_number()) |>   jsonlite::toJSON(dataframe = "rows", auto_unbox = TRUE)  RouterProvider(   Route(     path = "/",     element = div(Outlet()),     Route(       path = "people/:id",       loader = JS(sprintf(         "({ params }) => {           const db = %s;           const person = db.find(row => row.id == params.id);           if (!person) throw new Response('Not found', { status: 404 });           return person;         }",         people_json       )),       element = div(         useLoaderData(tags$h3(), selector = "name"),         useLoaderData(tags$span(), selector = "gender")       )     )   ) )

what: Injecting structured data into shiny.react components
title: Using useLoaderData
text: Pass shiny.react-based component specify target prop . loader data (selector field ) injected raw JavaScript value — converted string. needed whenever component expects actual array object prop, example rows data grid options autocomplete. loader returns object multiple fields, use selector extract relevant one:
code: library(reactRouter) library(muiDataGrid) library(htmltools)  loader <- JS("async () => {   const res = await fetch('https://swapi.info/api/people');   return (await res.json()).map((p, i) => ({     id: i, name: p.name, height: p.height   })); }")  RouterProvider(   Route(     path = "/",     loader = loader,     element = div(       style = "height: 400px;",       useLoaderData(         muiDataGrid::DataGrid(           columns = JS("[             { field: 'name',   headerName: 'Name',   flex: 1 },             { field: 'height', headerName: 'Height', width: 100 }           ]")         ),         as = "rows"       )     )   ) ) loader <- JS("async () => {   const res = await fetch('https://swapi.info/api/people');   const data = await res.json();   return {     names: data.map(p => p.name).sort(),     people: data.map((p, i) => ({ id: i, name: p.name, height: p.height }))   }; }")  RouterProvider(   Route(     path = "/",     loader = loader,     element = div(       useLoaderData(         muiMaterial::Autocomplete(           renderInput = JS(             "(params) => React.createElement(window.jsmodule['@mui/material'].TextField, {...params, label: 'Name'})"           ),           multiple = TRUE         ),         as = "options",         selector = "names"       ),       div(         style = "height: 400px;",         useLoaderData(           muiDataGrid::DataGrid(             columns = JS("[               { field: 'name',   headerName: 'Name',   flex: 1 },               { field: 'height', headerName: 'Height', width: 100 }             ]")           ),           as = "rows",           selector = "people"         )       )     )   ) )

title: Using useLoaderData
text: React Router community recommends loader returns exactly shape consuming component needs. avoids need selector: single route renders multiple components different data needs, consider splitting nested routes — loader:
code: # Loader returns the array directly — no selector needed loader <- JS("async () => {   const res = await fetch('https://swapi.info/api/people');   return (await res.json()).map((p, i) => ({     id: i, name: p.name, height: p.height   })); }")  useLoaderData(muiDataGrid::DataGrid(columns = columns), as = "rows") RouterProvider(   Route(     path = "/",     loader = stats_loader,     element = div(       useLoaderData(tags$h2(), selector = "summary"),       Outlet()     ),     Route(       index = TRUE,       loader = people_loader,       element = div(         style = "height: 400px;",         useLoaderData(muiDataGrid::DataGrid(columns = columns), as = "rows")       )     )   ) )

title: Using useLoaderData
text: useLoaderData() works component built shiny.react — simply injects prop value via React.cloneElement(). Examples compatible packages: muiDataGrid — MUI X Data Grid muiMaterial — MUI Material components muiCharts — MUI X Charts muiTreeView — MUI X Tree View shiny.fluent — Microsoft Fluent UI components shiny.blueprint — Palantir Blueprint UI components Standard htmltools tags (tags$div, tags$h3, etc.) support text injection (default = "children" structured data). muiCharts::BarChart — inject loader data dataset:
code: library(reactRouter) library(muiCharts) library(htmltools)  loader <- JS("async () => {   const res = await fetch('https://swapi.info/api/people');   const people = await res.json();   return people.slice(0, 10).map(p => ({     name: p.name,     height: Number(p.height) || 0,     mass: Number(p.mass) || 0   })); }")  RouterProvider(   Route(     path = "/",     loader = loader,     element = useLoaderData(       muiCharts::BarChart(         series = list(           list(dataKey = "height", label = "Height (cm)"),           list(dataKey = "mass",   label = "Mass (kg)")         ),         xAxis = list(list(scaleType = "band", dataKey = "name")),         height = 400       ),       as = "dataset"     )   ) )

title: Using useLoaderData
text: inject loader data one prop component, nest useLoaderData() calls: call extracts selector useLoaderData() result injects specified prop.
code: library(reactRouter) library(muiDataGrid) library(htmltools)  loader <- JS("async () => {   const res = await fetch('https://swapi.info/api/people');   const people = await res.json();   return {     columnDefs: [       { field: 'name',   headerName: 'Name',   flex: 1 },       { field: 'height', headerName: 'Height', width: 100 }     ],     people: people.map((p, i) => ({ id: i, name: p.name, height: p.height }))   }; }")  RouterProvider(   Route(     path = "/",     loader = loader,     element = div(       style = "height: 400px;",       useLoaderData(         useLoaderData(           muiDataGrid::DataGrid(),           as = "rows",           selector = "people"         ),         as = "columns",         selector = "columnDefs"       )     )   ) )

title: Using useLoaderData
text: useLoaderData() requires data router (createHashRouter, createBrowserRouter, createMemoryRouter) data routers support loader functions. work component-based routers (HashRouter, MemoryRouter). child component’s JavaScript bundle must loaded independently. example, muiDataGrid::DataGrid() automatically attaches HTML dependency — reactRouter bundle .

title: React Router for shiny Apps and Quarto
text: goal reactRouter provide wrapper around React Router (v6). [!CAUTION] Breaking Change v.0.1.2 : reloadDocument now FALSE default NavLink() Link(). Set reloadDocument = TRUE target routes contain Shiny server-rendered output like uiOutput() / renderUI().

title: React Router for shiny Apps and Quarto

code: library(reactRouter)  RouterProvider(   Route(     path = "/",     element = div(       NavLink(to = "/", "Main"),       NavLink(to = "/analysis", "Analysis"),       Outlet()     ),     Route(index = TRUE, element = "Main content"),     Route(path = "analysis", element = "Analysis content")   ) )

title: React Router for shiny Apps and Quarto
text: simplest way add routing RouterProvider(), defaults hash-based routing works Shiny apps, static HTML, Quarto documents: Lower-level functions (createHashRouter(), createMemoryRouter(), HashRouter(), MemoryRouter(), BrowserRouter()) also available control. See Choosing router vignette details code examples.
code: RouterProvider(   Route(path = "/", element = ..., Route(...)) ) # type = "hash" (default) — URL shows /#/about, bookmarkable # type = "memory"         — URL never changes, great for embedded widgets

title: Await — Await
text: https://reactrouter.com/6.30.0/components/await

title: Await — Await
code: Await(   into,   as = "children",   resolveKey,   selector = NULL,   errorElement = NULL,   fallback = NULL,   ... )

title: Await — Await
text: component (HTML tag shiny.react-based element) receive hook data specified prop. Character. name component's prop inject hook data (default "children" text display, "rows" data grid, "value" input). resolveKey Character. key loader's defer() return value holds promise (e.g. loader returns defer({ data: promise }), set resolveKey = "data"). selector Character. Optional key extract hook data object. NULL (default), entire data passed. errorElement Element render promise rejects. fallback Element shown promise pending. Defaults plain "Loading\u2026" span. ... Additional props pass component.

title: Await — Await
text: Renders deferred loader promise resolves, injecting resolved value (selector ) prop. Use inside Route whose loader returns defer() object (written via JS).

title: Link — Link
text: `reloadDocument` prop controls whether clicking link triggers full page reload (`TRUE`) client-side navigation (`FALSE`). default `FALSE`, matching React Router's default. Set `reloadDocument = TRUE` Shiny apps use server-rendered UI (`uiOutput`/`renderUI`) Shiny can re-initialize read new URL hash. https://reactrouter.com/6.30.0/components/link
